title: Dark solitons in the fractional NLS equation
authors: Almudena P. Márquez, Jesús Cuevas-Maraver, Panayotis G. Kevrekidis
abstract: In the present work we consider the subject of dark fractional solitary waves in the realm of generalized (fractional) forms of the nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation. While earlier studies have examined such states in the realm of real field theories, we showcase the existence and stability of individual dark solitary waves in such NLS settings and subsequently turn to two-soliton solutions. We find different branches of such two-soliton solution equilibria and contrary to the real field-theoretic setting all possible branches of two-soliton equilibria are found to be potentially unstable, although with different types of instabilities. Odd branches are potentially subject to oscillatory instabilities, while even branches are always exponentially unstable. The dynamics that results from the instabilities is also examined and is found to potentially feature breathing characteristics. This prompts us to seek and find associated periodic (breathing) orbits that are also unprecedented in this context, to the best of our knowledge. The effective particle-like dynamics of the solitary waves also prompts us to seek ordinary differential equation (ODE) descriptions to the dark soliton interaction dynamics. These are shown to hold promise toward providing us with effective reduced order models, indicating some potential directions for further investigation.
summary: This paper explores how fractional dispersion affects the stability and interaction of dark solitons. It reveals that unlike the standard NLS equation, all two-soliton bound states in the fractional regime are unstable, leading to complex behaviors like breathing orbits and soliton collisions.
main_problem: The study investigates the existence, stability, and dynamics of dark fractional solitary waves and their bound states (solitonic molecules) within the fractional Nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation.
main_result: The authors find that all branches of two-soliton equilibria in the fractional NLS setting are potentially unstable, exhibiting either oscillatory or exponential instabilities, and discover associated periodic breathing orbits.
method: Numerical analysis using Fourier spectral methods, Floquet analysis for periodic orbits, and a variational approach using a three-parameter ansatz to approximate stationary states.
model_or_system: A defocusing fractional Nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation utilizing the Riesz fractional derivative, which allows for continuous interpolation between different dispersive regimes via the Lévy index alpha.
key_observables: Soliton profiles, stability eigenvalues (real and imaginary parts), Floquet multipliers, and the time-evolution of density and projection onto unstable eigenvectors.
important_parameters: The Lévy index alpha (controlling dispersion), soliton separation/offset delta, and the frequency omega.
main_assumptions: The study assumes periodic boundary conditions (using soliton pairs to simulate single solitons), focuses on free space without external confinement, and uses an ansatz that fails to capture power-law tails.
figures_summary: Figures show dark soliton profiles across different alpha values, the dependence of equilibrium distance on alpha, eigenvalue spectra, and time-dependent density evolution showing breathing and collision dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fractional NLS model, analyzes single soliton profiles, investigates the stability and branches of two-soliton bound states, explores the resulting breathing and collision dynamics, and concludes with a semi-analytical variational approximation.
why_interesting: The discovery of unprecedented breathing orbits and the ability to model soliton interactions via reduced-order ODEs provides new insights into the complex dynamics of nonlinear waves in engineered dispersion landscapes, relevant to nonlinear optics and nonlinear wave physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Profile of dark solitons in linear (left) and logarithmic scale (right) for different values of α. Blue, red, yellow, purple, and green lines correspond, respectively, to α = 1.7, α = 2, α = 3, α = 3.5, and α = 4.

FIG. 2. Dependence of the equilibrium distance between the dark soliton centers in a bounded pair δ on the L´evy index α > 2. Notice the existence of two families (or three branches) interpolating between α = 2+ and α = 4. Additional branches emerge in the immediate vicinity of the biharmonic limit of α = 4, although given their exponential proximity to the biharmonic limit, we will not explore them here.

FIG. 3. (Left panel) Profiles of the bound states in the first branch for α = 3.5 (blue line), α = 3 (red line), α = 2.5 (yellow line), and α = 2.1 (purple line). (Right panel) Profiles of the bound states for the lowest (blue line), second (red line) and third (yellow line) branches at α = 3.8. Bottom panels show in logarithmic scales the profiles in the top panels.
FIG. 4. Dependence with α of the real (left panel) and imaginary (right panel) part of the spectrum for the lowest branch of bound states for α > 2.

FIG. 5. Dependence on α of the real part of the spectrum for the lower (left panel) and upper (right panel) branch of the second family of bound states. Dashed lines in the right panel correspond to a larger domain than full lines; in particular, the domain is [−L, L] with L = 320 and a discretization parameter h = 2/15.
